concerns.text: SC-002 states '≥90% of linear mixed-effects models must converge without simplification'. This is unmeasurable because the spec does not define the 'population' of models being tested (e.g., per-dataset, per-condition, per-participant). Without a defined denominator (N), a percentage cannot be calculated or verified.

SC-005 states 'post-hoc power ≥0.80 for N≥30 per condition'. This is circular and untestable as a success criterion. Post-hoc power is a deterministic function of the observed effect size and sample size. If the observed effect is small, power will be <0.80 regardless of N. The criterion conflates a design target (N≥30) with a result (power≥0.80), making it impossible to 'pass' if the data shows a null effect.

FR-007 requires 'post-hoc power analysis... to assess whether sample sizes... provide adequate power (≥0.80)'. This is methodologically unverifiable as a pass/fail requirement. If the analysis returns power <0.80, the requirement is 'failed', but the spec provides no procedure for what to do (e.g., collect more data, which is impossible in a retrospective analysis, or report the limitation). A requirement that can be 'failed' by the data itself without a remediation path is not a functional requirement.

Assumption 1 contains a nested conditional structure ('if not, If no dataset...') that is syntactically confusing and hard to verify. It mixes a fallback computation strategy with an exclusion criterion in a single sentence, making it difficult to write a deterministic test for the 'exclusion' logic.
